items.q: What happens during an El Nino event?
items.correct: Trade winds weaken, warm water that normally pools in the western Pacific spreads east, disrupting weather patterns globally — drought in Australia, floods in South America.
items.misconception: El Nino is when the ocean gets colder near Peru.
items.random_wrong: El Nino is the name of a specific hurricane that hit Mexico.
items.why: El Nino brings WARM water to Peru — the cold-water state is La Nina. Students mix up the two phases; the name 'the Christ child' refers to warm currents at Christmas.

items.q: Why is the Demographic Transition Model criticised for not applying to all countries?
items.correct: It was based on European industrialisation. LICs today face different conditions: imported medicine without industrialisation, cultural factors, gender inequality and globalised economies — not all follow the same path.
items.misconception: All countries follow the five stages of the DTM in order.
items.random_wrong: The DTM only applies to countries with more than 50 million people.
items.why: Many LICs experienced rapid death rate falls without industrialisation, breaking the model's logic. Some HICs are entering a Stage 5 it didn't predict — the DTM is a starting point, not a universal law.

items.correct: The process by which wealthier residents move into a previously low-income urban area, raising property prices and often displacing original residents.
items.misconception: Gentrification is when the government rebuilds an old part of the city.
items.random_wrong: Gentrification means the city becomes more polluted as it grows.
items.why: Students confuse gentrification (private market-led displacement) with regeneration (planned redevelopment). The key feature is who benefits and who is pushed out.

items.q: Which institute publishes the KOF Globalisation Index?
items.why: Correct answer: KOF Swiss Economic Institute (ETH Zurich). “IMF” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What does 'de facto' globalisation refer to?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Actual flows. “Legal frameworks” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In which year was the AT Kearney/Foreign Policy index first published?
items.why: Exact answer: 2001. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
